q: We do not believe him and neither does she. (Join the sentences, using ‘either...or’/ ‘neither...nor’.) (2025)
a: Neither we nor she believes him.

q: I was not involved in the project. He was not also involved in it. (Join the sentences, using ‘Neither... nor’) (2025)
a: Neither he nor I was involved in the project.

q: I did not fall while I was cycling. I did not break my leg while I was cycling. (Join the sentences, using ‘neither ... nor’.) (2025)
a: Neither did I fall nor break my leg while I was cycling.

q: They did not have enough food to eat. They did not have money to go to a restaurant. (Join the sentences, using ‘neither ... nor’.) (2025)
a: They had neither enough food to eat nor money to go to a restaurant.

q: The athlete did not win the race. He did not break his personal record. (Join the sentences, using ‘either...or’/’neither...nor’.) (2025)
a: The athlete neither won the race nor broke his personal record.

q: The blue dress is not available in larger sizes. The red dress is not available in larger sizes. (Join the sentences, using ‘either...or’/’neither... nor’.) (2026)
a: Neither the blue dress nor the red dress is available in larger sizes.

q: The guesthouse does not have air conditioning. It does not have Wi-Fi. (Join the sentences, using ‘either...or’/’neither...nor’.) (2026)
a: The guesthouse has neither air conditioning nor Wi-Fi.
